title: Making a simple outdoor planter box from treated softwood
subtitle: Build a square or rectangular timber planter using pressure-treated softwood boards, screwed and glued for outdoor durability
excerpt: How to make a simple outdoor planter box from pressure-treated softwood. Covers cutting the four sides and the base, assembling with exterior screws and waterproof glue, and lining or feet options for drainage.
sourceNotes: UK timber construction practice. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). Pressure-treated timber use guidance from the Timber Trade Federation.

glossaryTerms.definition: Softwood (usually Scots pine or spruce) impregnated with a preservative solution under pressure so the preservative penetrates the full thickness of the wood, not just the surface. The treatment makes the timber resistant to rot, fungi, and insect attack (typically for 15 to 25 years depending on the use class. Pressure-treated timber is green or brown when fresh; the colour fades to grey over one or two seasons. Cut ends expose untreated wood and should be painted with end-grain preservative. UC4 treatment is required for ground contact; UC3 for above-ground outdoor use.

content.text: is four sides screwed at the corners and a base with drainage holes. The technique is measuring, cutting, and assembling; it does not need a workbench beyond a pair of sawhorses. A 600 × 400 × 300 mm planter is a good starting size) stable, large enough for most herbs and vegetables, light enough to move when empty.

Use 22 mm thick pressure-treated boards in 150 mm width (a standard off-the-shelf size in most builders merchants. For a 600 × 400 mm internal plan: the two long sides are 600 mm long; the two short ends are 400 mm minus twice the board thickness (400 − 44 = 356 mm). The base is a single piece 560 mm × 356 mm cut from 18 mm exterior plywood. Adjust for your chosen size, keeping the maths consistent.

Mark all pieces with the combination square and carpenters pencil, then cut to length with the panel saw. Check each piece for square after cutting. Stack the four sides by size to check they are the right length before assembly. Paint all cut ends with end-grain preservative and allow to dry.

Stand a long side and a short end on edge at 90 degrees. Apply a bead of exterior wood glue (PVA or polyurethane) to the joining face of the short end. Clamp the corner square with a G-cramp if available; check the corner is 90 degrees with the combination square. Drive two 60 mm exterior screws through the face of the long board into the end of the short board) pre-drill the screw holes to avoid splitting. Repeat for all four corners. Fit the base with 40 mm screws from below (five or six screws into each side. Drill five drainage holes in the base, 12 mm diameter.

Raise the planter on three or four feet) 25 mm squares of offcut timber screwed to the base corners (so the base does not sit directly on a paved surface and wet rot cannot begin in pooled water. Line the inside with permeable landscaping membrane if using fine compost, to prevent soil washing through the drainage holes. Do not use impermeable plastic sheeting) it traps water against the timber and accelerates rot.
